name: Council of Nicaea Edits
description: The "Council of Nicaea Edits" theory posits that the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE, convened by Roman Emperor Constantine I, significantly altered the Bible and suppressed alternative Christian beliefs to consolidate the Emperor's power and create a unified, state-controlled religion. Proponents claim that texts contradicting the divinity of Jesus, promoting reincarnation, or emphasizing female deities were deliberately removed or rewritten. This narrative often includes accusations of a deliberate effort to hide pre-Christian pagan traditions absorbed into Christianity. The theory gained popularity through online forums, documentaries, and popular books, often drawing on interpretations of the Gnostic Gospels and apocryphal texts. Mainstream scholarship acknowledges the Council's role in defining Christian doctrine, particularly concerning the nature of Christ (the Nicene Creed), but rejects the notion of a systematic, malicious suppression of texts. Scholars argue that the canonization of the Bible was a gradual process spanning centuries, influenced by numerous factors beyond the Council's decisions, including theological debates, regional preferences, and the availability of manuscripts. While some texts were undoubtedly deemed heretical, this was driven by theological disagreements rather than a centralized conspiracy. The claim of Constantine's overwhelming influence is also debated, with historians pointing to the ongoing debates and regional variations in Christian practice for centuries after Nicaea.
subtitle: Alleged Biblical tampering by Constantine.

claims.claim: The Council of Nicaea selected the books of the Bible, removing others.
claims.evidence: References to apocryphal texts and Gnostic Gospels, perceived inconsistencies in the Bible.
claims.criticism: The canonization process was lengthy and decentralized, spanning centuries. The Council primarily addressed theological disputes regarding the nature of Christ, not biblical canon.

claims.claim: Constantine manipulated the Council to create a state religion.
claims.evidence: Constantine's political motives, his desire for a unified empire, his role in calling the Council.
claims.criticism: While Constantine undoubtedly had political motives, the theological debates were genuine and complex. The Council's decisions reflected a consensus among bishops, not solely Constantine's will.

claims.claim: Texts promoting reincarnation were removed from the Bible.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of certain biblical passages suggesting reincarnation, references to pre-Christian beliefs.
claims.criticism: The concept of reincarnation is largely absent from mainstream Christian theology, and there's no historical evidence to suggest it was a widespread belief within early Christianity. Interpretations are often based on selective readings of scripture.

keyFigures.name: Dan Brown
keyFigures.role: Popularized the theory through his novel 'The Da Vinci Code'.

keyFigures.name: Bart D. Ehrman
keyFigures.role: Biblical scholar who critiques the conspiracy theory in his writings.

timeline.event: Development of the biblical canon continues.
Emergence and popularization of the "Council of Nicaea Edits" theory.

sources.author: Bart D. Ehrman
sources.work: Misquoting Jesus: The Story Behind Who Changed the Bible and Why
sources.title: Bart D. Ehrman

sources.author: Philip Schaff

sources.title: Philip Schaff

sources.work: The Jesus Mysteries: Was the 'Original Jesus' a Pagan God?
sources.title: Timothy Freke & Peter Gandy
